items.question: She ___ in Girona since 2019.
explanations: 'Lived' is past simple, which suggests she doesn't live there any more. 'Since 2019' tells us the situation continues, so we need the present perfect: 'has lived'.
Correct! A situation that started in the past (2019) and continues now takes the present perfect, with 'since' + starting point.
'Lives' describes a general present fact, but 'since 2019' connects past and present, so English requires the present perfect: 'has lived'.
'Is living' is present continuous — it can't combine with 'since 2019' in standard English. Use the present perfect: 'has lived'.

items.question: I ___ that film yesterday.
explanations: 'Yesterday' is finished time, so the present perfect is impossible here — even though Spanish and Catalan allow it. Use the past simple: 'saw'.
Correct! With a finished-time expression like 'yesterday', English always uses the past simple.
Two problems: 'has' doesn't agree with 'I', and 'yesterday' requires the past simple anyway.
'See' is the base present form — it can't describe a completed action yesterday. Use 'saw'.

items.question: ___ you ever ___ sushi?
explanations: 'Ever' with life experiences calls for the present perfect, and after 'did' we'd need the base form ('try'), not 'tried' as a participle here.
'Have' is right, but the past participle of 'try' is 'tried', not 'tries'.
Correct! Life-experience questions use 'Have + subject + ever + past participle'.
'Do' makes a present simple question — but asking about a life experience needs the present perfect: 'Have you ever tried…?'

items.question: We've known each other ___ ten years.
explanations: 'Since' needs a starting point (since 2016, since university). 'Ten years' is a period, so use 'for'.
Correct! 'For' + a period of time (ten years). 'Since' would need a starting point.
'From' isn't used with the present perfect for duration. The pattern is 'for' + period.
'During' says when something happened inside a period, not how long it lasted. Duration takes 'for'.

items.question: He's ___ to the bank. He'll be back in ten minutes.
explanations: 'Been' means he went and has already come back — but the sentence says he'll be back later, so he's still there: 'gone'.
Correct! 'Gone' = he went and is still there. 'Been' would mean he has already returned.
After 'he's' (he has) we need a past participle, and the meaning 'still there' requires 'gone'.
'He's going' would be correct grammar for a future plan, but with 'He's + participle' expressing absence right now, we need 'gone'.

items.question: They ___ their homework yet.
explanations: 'Yet' points to 'up to now', which needs the present perfect, not the past simple.
Close — 'haven't' is right, but it must be followed by the past participle: 'finished'.
'Don't finish' is a present habit. With 'yet' we're talking about something expected before now: 'haven't finished'.
Correct! Negative present perfect + 'yet' at the end: 'They haven't finished their homework yet.'

items.question: I've worked here ___ I left university.
explanations: Correct! 'Since' can be followed by a clause that marks the starting point: 'since I left university'.
'For' needs a period (for five years), not an event that marks a starting point.
'When' would need a different structure; with present perfect + starting event, English uses 'since'.
'Until' marks an end point, but this sentence needs a starting point: 'since'.

items.question: Rewrite with the same meaning: 'I started living here in 2020 and I still live here.'
explanations: 'I lived here in 2020' suggests you don't live here now — the opposite of 'I still live here'.
Correct! Started in the past + continues now = present perfect with 'since' + starting point.
A very common error: the present simple can't combine with 'since 2020'. English needs the present perfect here.
'Had lived' is past perfect — it describes a situation before another past moment, not one continuing now.

items.question: She ___ her keys — she can't open the door.
explanations: Correct! The action is finished but the result matters now (she can't open the door) — classic present perfect.
'Lost' (past simple) is possible in isolation, but the second half stresses the present result, which the present perfect expresses best.
'Loses' would mean she loses her keys habitually — not what the sentence says.
'Is losing' describes an action in progress right now; losing keys is instantaneous. Use 'has lost'.

items.question: Which sentence means the same as 'This is the first time I have eaten paella'?
options: I have never eaten paella before.
I ate paella for the first time years ago.
I have eaten paella many times.
explanations: Correct! 'The first time I have eaten paella' = no previous experience = 'I have never eaten paella before (now)'.
'Already' says the experience happened before now at least once before this occasion — not a first time.
This puts the first time years in the past, contradicting 'this is the first time'.
'Many times' is the opposite of a first time.
